topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. "We regret to inform you that you have not been selected for the varsity squad." I reread the words three times, the screen blurring slightly. My hands started to tremble, a fine, uncontrollable vibration that made gripping my pen a genuine struggle. I slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet apartment. My breath hitched, coming in ragged, shallow gasps. I paced the small living room, my movements jerky and uncoordinated, eyes darting around the room as if searching for an escape route that didn't exist. A hot flush spread across my neck, and I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically squeeze the overwhelming pressure out of my skull. The world seemed too loud, too bright, and I couldn't focus on anything but the churning sensation in my stomach.

[story 2]

"No. No, this can't be happening." I whispered the words into the empty hallway, the sound thin and reedy. I stared at the acceptance letter, the ink swimming before my eyes. A sudden, intense wave of heat washed over me, starting in my chest and radiating outward. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the desk, and I could feel the frantic drumming of my heart against my ribs, a frantic, trapped thing. I started pacing the floor in tight, rapid circles, my vision tunneling. Every sound—the distant traffic, the hum of the refrigerator—became distorted, sharp edges of noise that clawed at my composure. I needed air, needed space, but the confines of the room felt suffocating. I started hyperventilating, sucking in deep, desperate breaths that did nothing to quell the rising tide of sheer disbelief and agony.

[story 3]

The coach’s voice, relayed through a terse phone call, had been delivered with such casual finality that it felt like a physical assault. I dropped the phone onto the hardwood floor; it didn't shatter, but the clatter was enough to snap something inside me. My entire body felt electrified, vibrating with a frantic energy that refused to settle. I stood stock-still for a moment, my jaw clenched so tightly I could taste copper. Then, the control fractured. I began to shake, a full-body tremor that made my limbs feel disconnected from my will. I walked to the window, leaning my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself, but the sheer magnitude of the disappointment was too large. Tears welled up, not slow, gentle tears, but sudden, hot surges that blurred my vision, making the landscape outside swim into an indistinct smear of color and shadow. I couldn't stop the shaking; it was just happening, an uncontrolled release of everything I was holding inside.
